text: Image and video processing are often conducted in the context of human perception. So understanding how an image is seen by the human eye is fundamental. Image quality assessment is a mathematical approach that tries to model and quantify how an image looks. Quality metrics quantify artifacts like noise and blur and work well within their assumption. They have only a loose relationship with human language and are not directly linked to the more subjective metrics that model how we feel when interacting with a certain visual content. Assessing more abstract notions about the feel of the visual content is traditionally done with supervised learning, where the training dataset is based on scores collected by showing images to people. This can be very expensive and time-consuming, which creates a very high cost on requiring only a minimum amount of information to be collected from users in order for the application to be as efficient as possible. In this paper we try to go a step further from traditional work and instead of only using a trained model we investigate how we can use knowledge included in an already trained model of a CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) algorithm that have a more general meaning on how an image or content should look, so that we do not need more data about the feeling people have about the quality of certain images, which is a costly data to be obtained from users, to obtain good results from the proposed model. We discuss possible prompts and show with experimental results that we have a method to design pairs of prompts that yields good results, for several commonly used quality assessment benchmarks and datasets.
